data.title: RSBT ETF: It's A Directional Bet, Not A Diversification Vehicle
data.description:
It's time to look at a potential diversification strategy by assessing the Return Stacked Bonds & Managed Futures ETF (RSBT). Various pivots have occurred in the global macro environment during recent years, and nearer-term reversions have shown signs of resurgence, making it an excellent time to review a vehicle such as RST, which has delivered price returns of ~12.25% year-over-year and currently has a trailing dividend yield of around 2.99%.

Herewith is my forward-looking take on the RSBT ETF.

What Is RSBT?

Figure 1 explains RSBT's strategy, which is to split roughly half of its portfolio into iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG), while investing the other half of its capital into managed futures strategies with the hope of producing higher risk-adjusted returns at an expense ratio of approximately 1.02% - distributions have been in the past, although sustainability beckons (distribution history available via this link). For those unaware, AGG ETF is an aggregate U.S. bond ETF weighted on supply; its current exposure includes: ~46% Treasuries, ~24% agency securities, +20% in financial & industrial corporate issuers, and residual allocations in sectors such as CMBS and utility issuers.

According to RSBT's material, its futures concept relies on external trend-following strategies. As a personal opinion, I can tell you that I've gained exposure to trend following during academia and later in-practice; futures can present momentum for a sustained period, but some have strong mean-reverting properties. Moreover, a futures book's macro exposure can be closely linked to your collateral base's macro exposure, which is self-defeating if your goal is diversification.

Figure 3 illustrates the fund's futures product universe. The selection pool is very streamlined, likely due to liquidity preferences. Ideally, I would've preferred seeing the fund exploit mispricing via long/short strategies as opposed to relying on trend. Long-Short relative value in futures can remove the macro-linkage between the underlying portfolio and the futures book. As things stand, I think trend following will expand the fund's beta as opposed to reducing it.

Figure 4 presents a view of the vehicle's current holdings. It's revealed that RPBT has long positions across the Treasury curve. Moreover, long exposure is held to second month Euro futures, Australian Dollar Futures, FTSE 100 futures, and S&P 500 E-Mini contracts. As previously mentioned, the fund's exposure changes rather frequently.

Lastly for this section, a time series of returns shows 12.25% in year-over-year gains and a cumulative loss of ~1.74% since-inception decline.

My Outlook

Headline & Correlation

The ETF's holdings aren't stagnant. However, as a means of illustration, I used its 2025 year-end reported holdings and created a pairwise analysis to assess correlation and covariance time-series - here's a list of the assets that I included (note that they were not weighted).

I started by forming a vector, where I ran a pairwise correlation and found a current 52-week rolling correlation of 0.34. Asset correlations broke down during 2025, presumably due to outliers, namely gold's illustrious run. Nonetheless, it can be seen from a throughout-the-cycle analysis that the positions rarely clustered. In fact, clusters during the pandemic and 2022's bear market didn't result in intra-asset values correlating anywhere near 1:1. While RSBT's holdings might change in time, I think we could reasonably conclude that the vehicle doesn't hold pure systematic exposure to a single asset class.

Assigning values to the vectors through covariances shows that volatility can cluster more vigorously than correlation in itself, proven by the pandemic. The covariance doesn't disprove correlation benefits, but it does warrant attention.

A direct reference to RSBT ETF's time series returns shows that, despite attempts at diversification, the managed futures strategy didn't add downside protection against the 2022/23 inflation-driven bond sell-off. Instead, returns decayed in excess (versus AGG). In contrast, we saw the vehicle outperform the AGG ETF strongly this year amid a rebound in bonds and outlying gains in precious metals.

What am I getting at?

I'm trying to say that we are likely looking at a higher beta version of the AGG ETF, which I suspect might be because of the vehicle's futures diversification strategy. It invests in a broad range of asset classes, but they are all macro-linked. As an example, the ETF's current futures portfolio (figure 4) basically bets on the same yield curve as the underlying bonds in the AGG ETF. The index positions in equities will likely add beta and not diversify during crisis regimes. FX and commodity diversification can soften downside beta but also lead to crash risk, seeing as the ETF's managed futures strategies are trend-following.

Assessing Its Hybrid Features

RSBT ETF's promotional material suggests that managed futures be held as a hybrid, by dividing a 60/40s bond exposure into 20% fixed income, 20% managed futures or by replacing bonds with managed futures entirely. While I can see the logic, I will highlight this: Past returns show that the RSBT ETF trades like a high-beta bond more than a portfolio convexity enhancer. Moreover, RSBT relies on trend-following in domains such as rates and FX, which are, by nature, mean-reverting. In my view, sticking to equity and precious metals trend-following would present better results. Alternatively, one could opt for relative value or discretionary strategies within asset classes, where you don't extract macro risk correlated to the underlying portfolio or run into crash risk issues.

My Directional Outlook (Based On Current Holdings)

I can only speak on behalf of the fund's current holdings when I provide a directional view. Based on my observations, I conclude the following.

Rates: I support the vehicle's current positioning in rates, where it holds a bullish view of Treasury futures in various parts of the curve. As communicated in my recent analysis of the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), I expect the yield curve to level down, providing support throughout the curve.

AUD/USD: The Australian dollar has delivered a ~10%+ return year-over-year versus the U.S. Dollar, largely on the basis of a rise in commodity prices. I see coin-integration with RSBT ETF's gold strategy in this trade. However, I think we could see foreign investors sustain mild momentum in the Australian dollar via short-term Carry trades; Australia has entered a re-inflationary trend, and its benchmark rates are currently at 385 basis points versus U.S. target rates of 350 - 375 basis points, and UK benchmark rates of 375 basis points (the UK has also entered disinflation).

FTSE & U.S. Equities: We are seeing sector rotation and stretched multiples in the S&P 500 and a trend from the FTSE 100. I'm not overly bullish or bearish about equity markets. However, I would highlight that they correlate during bad times, and although I acknowledge the strength of the momentum anomaly, I do not like the idea of trend-following at this time - regime shifts are occurring in equities - I cite "the great rotation."

I don't have directional opinions of the other asset classes available in RSBT ETF's selected pool. As a few mild observations, I believe the Euro to be a solid low-volatility currency alternative to the U.S. Dollar, but I don't have a directional view on it at the moment. I have a bullish long-run view of precious metals, but returns can be lumpy, and therefore, I don't like gold or silver as a managed futures strategy, at this stage.

As for asset classes that might be included ex-ante. I believe we could see Crude Oil trend-following being included in due course, given the multi-month rise in prices if both WTI and Brent. However, I don't like trading oil on trends, I prefer discretionary views, relative value, or spread trading, as a means of lowering portfolio beta and enhancing alpha.

Other Mentions

Dividends

Distributions have occurred in the past but details about future distribution plans remain sparse. I don't see this as a reliable proxy for AGG ETF, if the aim is to generate income.

Regime

I assessed the vehicle on a relatively short timeframe due to the vehicle's relatively short trading history. Since listing, the ETF has faced significant volatility from exogenous events, such as a 2022/23 sovereign debt market sell-off, a 2022 equity bear market and USD debasement. I think we'll see a few more regime changes in the next few years, which may present differentiated results from what we've seen from the RSBT ETF until now.

Conclusion

An analysis of the Return Stacked Bonds & Managed Futures ETF shows that it remains relatively cyclical, despite the correlation benefits derived from its managed futures strategy. Covariance can spike in certain regimes, largely because the ETF's managed futures overlay has similar macro-exposure as the fund's underlying position in AGG ETF, as opposed to style-based or discretionary exposure. Furthermore, the ETF exploits trend-following strategies, which can deliver robust returns for a sustained period. That being said, its trend following is exposed to mean reversion in rates and FX as well as crash risk in equities and precious metals.

Am I considering the RSBT ETF at this stage? Not quite; I don't see clear convexity gains from its strategy, mostly due to the reasons mentioned above. I think conviction in Treasuries, Agency Securities, and relative value futures would provide better portfolio risk utility than a trend follower with macro linkages between the underlying exposure and the futures overlay.

With all that being said, RSBT ETF has delivered stellar year-over-year returns. So, I might revisit my stance in the near future.

data.title: U.S. Goods Trade Deficit Hits All-Time High In 2025 - What Does It Mean For Q4 GDP?
data.description:
US trade deficit continued to widen in December after hitting the smallest monthly amount since the 2009 low in October. The total import was USD 70.3 billion higher than the export in the last month of 2025, according to the US Census Bureau.

Period 2025-12-01 2025-11-01 2025-10-01 2025-09-01 2025-08-01 2025-07-01 2025-06-01
Trade Balance -70.3 -53.0 -28.7 -47.7 -55.2 -73.9 -57.7
Exports 287.3 292.3 302.6 293.9 283.7 283.6 280.2
Imports 357.6 345.3 331.3 341.6 338.9 357.5 337.9

Now we have a full picture of the accumulated trade balance in 2025: compared to 2024, the full-year trade deficit for goods and services was merely USD 2.06 billion lower. The shortfall was unusually concentrated in the first half of the year as firms rushed to stock up on foreign products before the tariff policies hit.

If we zoom in to just goods trade, the accumulated goods trade gap rose to USD 1,230 billion in 2025, from USD 1,204 billion in the previous year, reaching a new all-time high.

What do these trade data mean for Q4 GDP

The December figures also give us a complete overview of Q4 net export contribution to economic growth. Now, to translate trade numbers into GDP contributions, one of the most important adjustments these days is to first exclude non-monetary gold.

This is because most of the cross-border trade involving non-monetary gold involves no local production in the US; they are merely ownership transfers of investment assets that are not within GDP’s coverage.

Removing non-monetary gold from trade data, the trade deficit for goods and services in December would rise to USD 73 billion.

Take the average of the month of ex-gold trade deficit in Q4 (shown in the red lines in the graph below), and we can see the number was very close to the level in Q3. This means we should expect a minimal contribution from net exports in the Q4 GDP.

This is also the same conclusion the Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow draws. The nowcasting tool degraded its Q4 GDP growth estimate to 3%, with the expected contribution from net exports falling to merely 0.02 percentage points (pps), from 0.62 pps in the second most recent update.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: FX Outlook: USD/CHF Carves A Bottom After Reaching 14-Year Lows
data.description:
By Elior Manier

2025 was the year of the Swiss franc, and there were quite a few reasons. De-dollarization, flows moving towards Europe, the yen losing some of its safe-haven characteristics amid fiscal trouble in Japan, and general diversification towards quality as the world faces troubled times ahead.

Reaching 14-year lows less than a month ago, USD/CHF had become a bear dream for those who thrive on dollar outflows. 2025 began with a 17% decline in the major pair and was at the center of the essential themes driving FX flows.

But as dollar bearish positioning also reaches decade lows, some questions regarding the extended moves are arising. If the US dollar finds reasons to catch a serious bid, troubled times could be coming ahead for heavy Greenback-Short sellers. A question evoked in our recent dollar index analysis.

Switzerland is a victim of quite heavy deflationary pressures, with its currency strengthening (hurting Swiss exports) and the rougher US trade policies, which exacerbate a fundamentally low-inflation regime.

The latest inflation report showed a modest 0.1% increase, but if inflation doesn't show a material increase in the coming few reports, the Central Bank might be forced to turn to negative rates, and such come at high costs.

In the meantime, USD/CHF has carved out a pretty strong bottom in recent days and could offer interesting mean-reversion setups for those looking for FX volatility ahead.

Let's dive right into a multi-timeframe analysis of USD/CHF to spot where the action stands and where it could be heading.

USD/CHF Multi-Timeframe Technical Analysis

Daily Chart

The daily RSI and uptrend are working together to provide a fresh push in the pair, with traders leaning on the Monday lows (0.7675) to drive the action higher.

  • If the action falls back below the weekly lows, the breakout will be void but for now it gives high probabilities of holding.

4H Chart and Technical Levels

Trading Levels for USD/CHF

Resistance Levels

  • 0.7780 to 0.78 momentum pivot
  • 0.7850 2025 lows pivotal resistance (bullish above)
  • 0.7950 minor resistance
  • 0.8075 to 0.81 late 2025 range highs

Support Levels

  • 0.7725 50H moving average
  • 0.77 to 0.7725 August 2011 lows support
  • 0.76292 2026 and 14-year lows
  • 0.76 support zone July 2011
  • 0.70696 all-time lows (August 2011)

1H Chart

  • Traders looking to capture a potential bull move could wait for a retracement to the 50-hour moving average at 0.77250.
  • If the action doesn't pull back lower, traders can look for breakout setups
    • Breaching the February high of 0.78175 would point to a swift test of the pivotal resistance around 0.7850.
  • Any daily close below the 200-hour MA could prompt a retest of the 2026 low and further bearish action, but technicals aren't pointing to such outcomes for now.

Safe Trades!

data.title: Small Caps Stage Quiet Comeback As AI Trade Shows Cracks
data.description:
By Jim Iuorio

After years in the shadow of big tech, small-cap stocks are outperforming, and the reasons suggest the trend may have staying power. The Russell 2000's four-month outperformance against mega-cap tech represents one of 2025's most significant but underappreciated market rotations.

In November, concerns around stretched valuations began to grow, and on November 20, E-mini Nasdaq-100 (NQ) options volume marked their third highest volume day as traders hedged and navigated tech uncertainty following Nvidia earnings and Non-Farm Payrolls (NFP) data.

Since November 21, the small-cap Russell 2000 index has surged more than 16% and touched record highs on January 22. Over the same period, the Nasdaq climbed just 8%. The S&P 500 hasn't kept pace either, despite reaching its own new highs in late January.

The shift marks a striking reversal from the previous three years, when artificial intelligence (AI) euphoria drove the Nasdaq up 145% while small caps languished. To fully understand the mechanics of this trade, let’s go back to the beginning.

Years of Divergence

In early 2023, the Nasdaq Composite began what would become a historic rally, surging 145% in less than three years. The advance was remarkable not just for its magnitude, but for its narrow leadership. A small group of mega-cap technology companies, particularly those at the forefront of the AI evolution, drove the bulk of the gains.

To illustrate the magnitude of this move: When the Nasdaq reached its all-time high in November 2023, the S&P 500 trailed far behind with a gain of 79%, and the Russell 2000 lagged even further behind with a modest 43% gain. The tailwind for the Nasdaq was widely reported and dominated financial media headlines, while Russell's underperformance received less attention.

The Case Against Small Caps

The fundamental case for small-cap underperformance was multifaceted and, for a time, compelling. The most pressing concern centered on interest rates. In January 2023, the Federal Reserve's benchmark rate stood at 4.25%, already elevated by recent historical standards. But with inflation still running hot, markets expected further tightening – and got it. By July 2023, the Fed had raised rates to 5.25%, adding 100 basis points of pressure.

Stocks included in the Russell tend to be extremely sensitive to short-term rates. Many of these smaller companies use revolving rates to conduct business, unlike cash-rich entities like Apple (AAPL) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL).

Economic uncertainty added another layer of concern. A disappointing 1.3% GDP reading in December 2022 sparked widespread fears of an imminent recession. Although those fears would prove premature – the recession never materialized – the anxiety lingered for several quarters, keeping investors cautious about economically sensitive small-cap stocks.

This did not seem to stop traders from favoring the Nasdaq over the Russell. Even when the Fed pivoted to cutting rates in the fall of 2024, delivering 100 basis points of easing, the outperformance of the Nasdaq persisted.

By then, the narrative appeared to have shifted. Rate sensitivity mattered less than the seemingly unstoppable momentum behind artificial intelligence and the mega-cap stocks positioned to capitalize on it.

A Change in Conditions

By late 2025, however, three significant developments began to alter the equation. First, the Fed resumed its easing cycle with 75 basis points of cuts. What makes these rate reductions particularly notable is their timing: they are occurring against a backdrop of economic expansion, not weakness.

The Fed's focus appears to be the labor market, where recent data have shown softening despite otherwise healthy economic indicators. Under its dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment, the central bank is responding to labor market weakness even as other measures suggest the economy remains on solid footing.

Second, the Fed appears to be anticipating that the same AI that’s eliminating jobs might also combat inflation through productivity gains. If true, 2026 could bring a Goldilocks scenario of moderate economic growth, low unemployment and stable inflation – especially with potential tailwinds from tax cuts, deregulation and reshoring investment. Small caps are more sensitive to these domestic factors, as they are less likely than large caps to be doing business across the globe.

Third, AI exuberance itself is showing signs of fatigue. Nvidia (NVDA) shed 20% in early November, and the entire Mag 7 lost 10%. While these are minor moves when viewed against their enormous multi-year rallies, they raise a critical question: Has this combination of factors provided enough incentive to cause a rush of liquidation in the long Nasdaq/short Russell trade?

If the answer is yes, and that liquidation has indeed begun, it suggests a path for continued outperformance of the Russell into 2026.

Accessing the Trade

For investors interested in expressing this view, pair trading with Equity Index options enables traders to express relative performance views between indices while maintaining defined risk parameters regardless of overall market direction.

The strategy's effectiveness hinges on thoughtful position construction, where traders must choose between dollar-neutral weighting – which equalizes notional exposure – or volatility-adjusted weighting that accounts for the historical behavior differences between indices like the Russell 2000 and Nasdaq-100.

By utilizing liquid CME Group Equity options contracts, market participants can structure trades that isolate their conviction about one index outperforming another, transforming directional market views into actionable positions with clear maximum loss thresholds.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Annaly Capital's Easy Money Is Gone - But The 12% Yield Isn't
data.description:
In May 2025, I had rated Annaly Capital Management (NLY) as a Hold, leaning toward a Buy (Hold for the short term, accumulation for long term investors). I did see positives ahead, as yield curve steepening would improve spreads and dividend durability looked intact. As things transpired, markets discounted a recovering macro environment ahead of time, and May of last year was indeed the best time to buy NLY, even from a short-term lens. NLY retained its high-yield profile over the next few quarters and importantly, has seen ~33% total returns too (price appreciation of ~20%).

With an economic return of ~8% in Q4 alone, NLY closed a great year in 2025, where economic returns were above 20%. Q4 also saw a 5% sequential rise in book value. EAD was again sufficient to cover a lucrative yield of above 12% (annualizing ~$0.7 in dividends paid in Q4). Even economic leverage is conservative at ~5.6x. Overall, the inflection I thought would take some time but emerge over a longer term, has arrived earlier than expected.

The forward question changes, though. The markets seem to have priced in the inflection to a large extent. With spreads already tight and valuations now at a premium (was trading at a discount in May last year), NLY appears to be a Hold with most of the gains ahead driven by the income component rather than book value expansion or further valuation rerating.

Spread Environment

The Fed cut rates by 75 bps in 2025, and short-term borrowing costs declined. This meant Annaly's average repo rate also improved by 30 bps to ~4.2% for the full year (4.02% in Q4). So, in 2025, net interest spread (ex-PAA) was roughly 1.4%, up by almost 18 bps. These are the basic mechanics explaining the solid EAD covering a lucrative dividend and a strong full year economic return, we noted earlier. Additionally, 2025 also meant lower rate volatility, lower hedging costs, and tighter mortgage spreads, helping lift asset prices and hence a stronger book value.

The current environment is stable but does not seem to indicate the same kind of inflection ahead. Repo costs remain stable near ~4%, supported by improved funding conditions. But Agency spreads are now near the tighter end of their historical range. That means the large tailwind from spread compression has already occurred. Which is why I feel this is an environment where carry stability will dominate NLY's thesis, not another round of repricing. According to management commentary in Q4 2025,

So the Agency market is a safe place right now. It's just that spreads are obviously at the tight end of the range. They're close to QE type levels. The safety of those returns is there, but the abundance of yield is not quite there.

Dividend Anchored in Spread Economics

For long-term investors, it is important to understand that structurally NLY's dividend policy implies a huge dependency on a supportive spread regime (with a ~95% payout ratio). The yield therefore fluctuates significantly, and payouts could be revised, particularly if the EAD drops structurally (not a single quarter temporary fall). If funding costs rise or Agency spreads compress further from already tight levels, EAD could decline.

There is no immediate cause of visible concern, though. With leverage at a moderate 5.6x and liquidity representing roughly 58% of total capital, the current balance sheet does not indicate stress. Management also stated they expect to out-earn the dividend in Q1, which supports near-term stability.

Portfolio Shift Indicates Stability

The dividend yield variability tied to a cyclical Agency spread environment is likely to be addressed to an extent by NLY's portfolio shift toward more Residential Credit and MSR (which will reduce the Agency mix from 62% to 50%). This should smooth cash flows and provide a return stream that is more diversified away from Agency MBS tightening. These newer areas of emphasis introduce different variables like borrower quality and servicing behavior and reduce NLY's EAD dependence on funding spreads and rate volatility alone.

On the credit side, this expansion looks cushioned with disciplined underwriting (762 FICO, 68% LTV) rather than yield chasing. In the MSR portfolio too, the current position appears healthy. With mortgage rates well above the portfolio's 3.28% weighted average note rate, borrowers are roughly 250 bps out of the money. That means refinancing incentives are low. As a result, prepayment risks are lower (CPR at 4.6%), and servicing fees should continue to add up and keep EAD steadily supported. In this setup, MSR behaves like a stable, recurring income stream. This ensures that a tight spread in the Agency segment is not the central point of focus.

MSR is not designed to drive higher earnings in favorable spread environments, but to provide balance - offering steady income when rates are high and acting as a partial hedge against Agency convexity risk when rates fall - smoothing performance across different rate regimes.

Overall, the portfolio shifts are not necessarily EAD-boosting. Agency assets, when spreads are favorable and leverage is applied, can generate stronger immediate carry. So, emphasis on MSR and Residential Credit is more about smoother than larger EAD ahead. This does not ensure dividends are protected, but fluctuations will be far fewer.

Valuation and Risks

Having discussed the business' health, let us now turn our attention to the stock. Compared to May last year, the most significant change for the stock has been a rerating toward historically high premiums in terms of price-to-book value. The high valuations coincide with an environment where risks are not at their lowest. Management highlighted risks from a global debt complacency and asset valuation euphoria. A G-fee cut could also impact the prepayment environment.

Looking at the balance of valuations and risks at play today (valuations are more sensitive to shocks now), I feel NLY should be best treated as a Hold and viewed as a carry position, where most of the heavy lifting ahead is done by the yield. The current indicators do not show any potential and immediate stress on the EAD.

Long-term investors can continue to Hold NLY and keep an eye on Agency MBS spread widening, any macro signs of repo stress, rate volatility, and even intra-quarter book value updates, if any, to trim positions. Short term investors, who do not have the outlook to ride full economic cycles, will have to take valuations into account as well. There too, current valuations do not support fresh capital deployment. So unless you are already invested, it is best to wait for share prices to return toward book value, or for a spread widening environment that improves asset entry yields.

data.title: FXR: Sophisticated Strategy With A High Fee Lagging XLI, A Hold
data.description:
I believe that while this year's capital rotation to cyclicals is a phenomenon that is impossible to ignore, the First Trust Industrials/Producer Durables AlphaDEX® Fund ETF (FXR) is a suboptimal vehicle to bet on this shift to continue further into 2026 and thus reap benefits from industrials' rally.

My stance is driven by the fact that FXR has a history of consistent underperformance not only vs. the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) but also vs. the State Street® Industrial Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLI), which is quite disappointing assuming substantial differences in their expense ratios and that FXR's strategy incorporates sophisticated screening and factor considerations. And while this ETF does have a few advantages over XLI (chiefly in terms of value factor exposure), I believe it does not qualify for a Buy rating.

FXR Strategy And Portfolio

As we know from the First Trust website, incepted in 2007, FXR tracks the StrataQuant® Industrials Index. The index calibration process is rather sophisticated, with multiple steps involved. As explained in the fact sheet, the process starts with the Russell 1000® Index:

IDI constructs the StrataQuant® Industrials Index by ranking the stocks which are members of the Russell 1000® Index on growth factors including three, six and 12-month price appreciation, sales to price and one year sales growth, and, separately, on value factors including book value to price, cash flow to price and return on assets. All stocks are ranked on the sum of ranks for the growth factors and, separately, all stocks are ranked on the sum of ranks for the value factors.

In the next step, it is decided whether a stock belongs to a value or a growth cohort or is something in-between:

Each stock receives either its growth or value score rank as its selection score based on its style designation as determined by Russell. Stocks which Russell allocates between both growth and value receive the better of their growth or value score rank as their selection score.

After that, names with the highest scores from the respective sector join the index:

IDI then ranks those stocks contained in the industrials/producer durables sector according to their score. The greater of the top 75% of the eligible universe or 40 stocks is selected for the StrataQuant® Industrials Index.

Ultimately, I should also highlight that the weighting scheme applied is also far from simple:

The selected stocks are divided into quintiles based on their rankings and the top ranked quintiles receive a higher weight within the index. The stocks are equally-weighted within each quintile.

So given such rigorous screening and complicated weighting, I am not surprised that FXR has a 76% turnover, especially considering the index is not just rebalanced but also reconstituted quarterly. To contextualize, XLI's turnover is just 3%.

FXR vs. XLI: Portfolio Composition

What is inside the FXR portfolio, and does it demonstrate exposures compelling enough to favor it over XLI? I have identified a few advantages it does offer, mainly in terms of the value factor, yet they are not strong enough to justify a bullish stance.

More specifically, my analysis shows that the product of this complex methodology is a portfolio of 145 equities that has an overlap with XLI but not the one I would call substantial, as only 30.37% of its net assets are allocated to companies that qualified for the Industrial Select Sector Index (hence, for XLI). Interestingly, in the XLI portfolio, these names play a much greater role, accounting for 67% of the net assets. And this is not the consequence of just rigorous factor screening.

The issue here is more nuanced: all the stocks present in the FXR portfolio belong to the Industrials industry (and the seven sectors within it, from aerospace and defense to industrial transportation) in the Industry Classification Benchmark system, which is maintained by FTSE Russell. But the methodology the Industrial Select Sector index relies on is the Global Industry Classification Standard, which is maintained by MSCI Inc. (MSCI). And when the GICS is applied to the FXR portfolio, we can, to our surprise, find out that there are five sectors in it instead of one: it also features consumer discretionary, financials, IT, and materials.

Sector Weight, as of February 13
Industrials 67.27%
Materials 16.31%
Financials 8.82%
Information Technology 4.22%
Consumer Discretionary 3.27%

Calculated by the author using data from FXR and Seeking Alpha

Looking at the key ten group with Comfort Systems USA, Inc. at the very top, we see that the similarities between it and the top 10 cohort of XLI are scarce.

Company Ticker Weight in FXR, as of February 13 Weight in XLI, as of February 13 EV/EBITDA TTM GICS Sector Market Cap, $ Billion, as of February 17
Comfort Systems USA, Inc. (FIX) 1.46% 0.88% 37.26 Industrials 47.09
FTAI Aviation Ltd. (FTAI) 1.44% N/A 32.86 Industrials 28.71
Oshkosh Corporation (OSK) 1.38% N/A 9.79 Industrials 10.82
Smurfit Westrock Plc (SW) 1.35% N/A 8.28 Materials 26.86
EMCOR Group, Inc. (EME) 1.33% 0.67% 20.84 Industrials 35.85
FedEx Corporation (FDX) 1.32% 1.52% 10.48 Industrials 88.11
DuPont de Nemours Inc. (DD) 1.27% N/A 15.35 Materials 20.55
MasTec, Inc. (MTZ) 1.26% N/A 23.21 Industrials 20.93
Quanta Services, Inc. (PWR) 1.26% 1.46% 33.94 Industrials 78.15
GE Vernova Inc. (GEV) 1.25% 4.07% 69.98 Industrials 216.20

Created by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha

For instance, five names have not qualified for the XLI portfolio, given they are not S&P 500 constituents (but they must be to join the index XLI tracks), while DD did not make it to the index as it is a materials company in the GICS system. And Caterpillar Inc. (CAT), one of the leaders in the construction machinery & heavy transportation equipment industry, which is the most significant holding of XLI with around 6.8% allocated, is only in 20th place in FXR, having just 1.1% weight.

What we also see in the table above is that a few of these names have quite stretched EV/EBITDA (Trailing Twelve Months) ratios—for the industrials sector, the median multiple is 14.96x, and for materials, it is even lower at 12.1x.

Still, this is just the first impression, and deeper research does not confirm the expensiveness of FXR's portfolio.

As we can immediately tell from FXR's over 27% allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation rating or higher, its portfolio is, in fact, much less expensive than those of XLI and IVV. The same message is conveyed by the adjusted weighted average earnings yield and other metrics. One the contributors here is that FXR has considerably larger exposure to SMID stocks (names with market capitalizations below $10 billion), with close to a third of the net assets allocated to them.

Next, growth, which is, by and large, the antipode of value, is where the FXR portfolio is noticeably weaker than both these names.

Interestingly, FXR is also underweight in momentum names (those with a Quant Momentum grade of B- or higher).

As to the GARP characteristics, the picture is mixed, as FXR has a lower PEG ratio (calculated with the weighted average TTM P/E in the numerator and forward EPS growth rate in the denominator) than XLI, but its EV/EBITDA-to-Growth ratio is nonetheless larger.

Metric FXR XLI IVV
PEG Ratio 2.264 2.522 1.239
Price/Sales-to-Growth Ratio 0.533 0.615 0.539
EV/EBITDA-to-Growth Ratio 2.260 2.136 1.043

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

And compared to IVV, both its PEG and EV/EBITDA-to-Growth ratios are substantially higher, while the PSG ratios are on par.

Regarding quality, I would not say that the sophisticated selection and weighting methodology of the index FXR tracks translated here into meaningful advantages over XLI, which has a much simpler approach. It still has a higher adjusted Return on Equity (with negative and triple-digit figures removed), yet its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability rating or higher, a parameter I prioritize over individual weighted average metrics, is the smallest in the group.

Importantly, even if the index that lies at the crux of XLI applied an equal weighting scheme instead of the modified market cap weighting, the portfolio's allocation to stocks that earned no less than a B- Profitability grade would be close to 95%, as my model shows.

FXR Performance

With a 60 bps expense ratio, FXR is anything but an inexpensive vehicle. For context, XLI has an ER of just 8 bps. Besides, I did further research on whether expenses were even higher in the past, and using the Wayback Machine that helped me to find the version of its web page saved on August 15, 2007, I found out that back then (and FXR was incepted in May 2007), its gross ER was 0.89% and the net ER was 0.70%.

But considering expenses that high, investors might expect encouraging performance. And here, there is a problem: beating XLI, the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), and IVV has been quite problematic for FXR (except for quite rare periods, including YTD).

Optimists can make a point though that this year FXR has been doing excellently, with a price return above those of XLI, the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF, and IWB.

Next, with 18 full calendar months in the books, FXR outperformed XLI in 9, which is a decent result on the surface.

However, delving deeper, we see that FXR has still underperformed XLI (which, by the way, has recently closed the performance gap with the S&P 500 ETF) by over 131% since its inception in May 2007, also trailing IVV and IWB.

Besides, my calculations show that in its 9 successful calendar years, the median active return (with XLI selected as a benchmark) was 4.3% vs. -5.46% in the weak years.

So, for the June 2007–January 2026 period, it delivered an annualized total return 1.38% lower than XLI's and 1.72% lower than IVV's, capturing close to 131% of the downside of the latter (and 110.68% of the former), while also delivering the highest standard deviation in the group.

Final Thoughts

Even though FXR is beating the State Street® Industrial Select Sector SPDR® ETF this year, given its history of underperformance, deep maximum drawdowns, substantially higher expense ratio, and a 10x larger 30-day median bid/ask spread (which was at 0.10% as of February 18), it is quite complicated to justify why investors should favor it over XLI, regardless of whether they are going to tactically cut exposure to the tech-heavy S&P 500 ETF to play the capital rotation narrative or find a vehicle for holding long-term with a strategic purpose in mind. In this regard, while I find FXR's factor mix quite compelling in terms of value factor exposure and the strategy certainly sensible, I would gravitate toward a neutral view, which equals the Hold rating.

data.title: Fortress Biotech: The PRV Catalyst That Could Reprice This Microcap
data.description:
Fortress Biotech (FBIO) is a company that mostly acquires and develops drugs and biologic treatments through subsidiaries, partner companies, and external collaborations. FBIO's dermatology assets are developed through Journey Medical (DERM), which markets a set of branded products like the recently approved Emrosi for rosacea. FBIO retains its longer-dated optionality across late-stage and earlier programs through equity and royalties. This way, FBIO still has exposure to diverse areas like oncology, rare diseases, infectious illnesses, transplant immunology, pain treatments, and metabolic conditions. More importantly, in January 2026, they secured a valuable PRV, which I feel could quickly unlock significant shareholder value. Thus, I feel FBIO's risk-reward at this point now favors the bulls at these levels.

Dermatology And Rollup M&A

Fortress Biotech is, at its core, a life sciences company that works on dermatology treatments. However, they also acquire other drug and biologic programs in multiple therapeutic areas to advance them through partnerships and subsidiaries. FBIO was founded back in 2006 and is currently headquartered in Bay Harbor Islands, Florida. Having said that, FBIO's business model differs from most other biotechs as it's explicitly focused on trying to acquire late-stage assets or already approved drugs in foreign jurisdictions. So, in that sense, FBIO is not a pure play on biotech R&D but rather a bet on management's insights on the state of the art in biotech and their ability to execute on their acquired candidates.

As a quick overview, note that FBIO's portfolio includes Emrosi, which is a tetracycline-class oral therapy for inflammatory lesions of rosacea in adults. This product was developed by Journey Medical, a partner focused on dermatology, owned 37% by FBIO. Other dermatological offerings in FBIO's portfolio are Amzeeq and Zilxi, also for rosacea, acquired from VYNE Therapeutics Inc. (VYNE), and Qbrexza, indicated for hyperhidrosis, acquired from Dermira, an Eli Lilly (LLY) subsidiary. Additionally, the following drugs are commercialized by DERM: Accutane and Targadox for acne, Exelderm for fungal and dermatological infections, and Luxamend for wound and skin care.

Likewise, FBIO owns CUTX-101, which is another commercial product (also known as Zycubo). This is a copper replacement therapy for Menkes disease in pediatric patients. The FDA approved CUTX-101 on January 13, 2026, and it was initially developed by FBIO's subsidiary Cyprium Therapeutics. Later, Cyprium completed an asset transfer to Sentynl, which is how they retain 3.0%-12.5% of royalties and other participations from this particular asset.

Late Stage Pipeline

Moreover, it's important to highlight that FBIO's pipeline has several late-stage candidates. One of them is CAEL-101 (anselamimab), a monoclonal antibody studied in Phase 3 for AL amyloidosis therapy by Caelum. This company was a former subsidiary of FBIO that was acquired by AstraZeneca (AZN) in 2021. And FBIO can eventually receive up to $125 million after reaching certain R&D and commercial milestones. Unfortunately, its recent Phase 3 trial didn't actually achieve statistical significance for the primary endpoint in the overall population. FBIO believes there are still some subgroup signals that remain promising and are under evaluation, but it's clearly no longer a major value driver for FBIO at this point.

Another one of FBIO's late-stage candidates is Dotinurad, which is a uric acid transporter 1 inhibitor, used to lower serum uric acid for gout and hyperuricemia. This asset is much more promising, as it's already approved in some Asian countries and has advanced into global Phase 3. Dotinurad was developed by Urica Therapeutics, a company majority-owned by FBIO. Urica sold Dotinurad to Crystalys Therapeutics in 2024, but FBIO retains upside exposure through a minority position in Crystalys. Plus, FBIO remains eligible for 3% on royalty payments contingent on future net sales of Dotinurad.

Likewise, another late-stage asset is Triplex. This one is a viral-vector vaccine designed to generate T-cell responses against cytomegalovirus (CMV) complications, which is especially important in immunocompromised settings like transplantation. Helocyte, an FBIO subsidiary, is developing this particular candidate. So far, this vaccine is in Phase 2 for CMV control in liver transplant recipients, in Phase 1/2 in pediatric hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) recipients, and in Phase 2 in adults living with HIV and CMV. FBIO also has a trial that tests Triplex in combination with a bispecific CAR-T in non-Hodgkin lymphoma. As with Dotinurad, FBIO is also eligible for payments upon milestone achievements.

Early-to-Mid Stage Pipeline

Lastly, it's also worth mentioning that FBIO is working on earlier-stage assets as well, though these are undoubtedly inherently more speculative. Concretely, FBIO has programs with economic participation through equity and royalties, such as CMV-HIV CAR-T, a bispecific CAR-T, which is enrolling for a Phase 1 trial. Likewise, FBIO's Mustang Bio programs for recurrent glioblastoma and AJ201 for spinal and bulbar muscular atrophy (SBMA) are promising R&D projects.

Furthermore, FBIO also has IV tramadol for postoperative acute pain, as well as preclinical candidates like FB-606 for Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Other interesting pre-clinical drugs are AAV-SH for complement-mediated diseases and AAV-ATP7A for Menkes gene therapy. But, naturally, these assets aren't necessarily going to reach regulatory approval any time soon, as developing them will likely take several years. However, this doesn't mean that FBIO can't monetize them before reaching regulatory approval.

For example, in Q2 2025, FBIO sold Checkpoint to Sun Pharma, and received $28 million upfront with $4.8 million more tied to a Contingent Value Right (CVR) linked to milestones in the European approval. On top of that, FBIO is eligible for a 2.5% royalty on net sales. A Checkpoint product is Unloxcyt (cosibelimab) for cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC), which has a BLA approved in December 2024. So, FBIO's business model can definitely result in lumpy revenues due to these types of opportunistic asset sales as well.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, FBIO currently trades at a $108.6 million market cap, so it remains decisively in microcap territory. This obviously makes it inherently riskier because it's a thinly traded stock with only 199.3 thousand shares in volume. At the current $3.45 PPS, that implies a shallow trading liquidity of just $687.6 thousand, meaning that even tiny buy/sell orders can significantly push the stock price.

However, according to Seeking Alpha's dashboard on FBIO, the company is actually projected to generate around $109.5 million in revenues this year (likely tied to a potential PRV sale; more on this later). That would price FBIO at a very attractive 1.0 P/S. For comparison, their sector's median P/S is around 3.6, so on this metric alone, FBIO already appears quite undervalued. Also, consider that FBIO's Q3 2025 balance sheet held only $86.2 million in cash, against $53.4 million in financial debt (i.e., notes payable) aside from other regular operating liabilities. So that implies an even cheaper EV of about $75.8 million, which would translate to a very compelling EV/S of 0.7. Again, this is significantly lower than its sector's median EV/S of 3.7.

Nevertheless, FBIO is not without its drawbacks at this stage. I also calculate that FBIO's Q3 2025 burned through only $6.1 million. Note that I got this figure by adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX. But, on a TTM basis, FBIO's cash burn is actually markedly higher at approximately $66.1 million (using the same approach). And, under that more stringent cash burn estimate, FBIO's cash runway is only around 1.3 years. This is a manageable runway, but it does show that FBIO's business model isn't self-sustainable yet, which is why I can't quite rate it any higher than a speculative "Buy" at best for now.

But, ultimately, what nudges me towards a speculative "Buy" is that they have a concrete bullish catalyst in the near term. In particular, FBIO recently received a Priority Review Voucher (PRV) tied to my previously mentioned Cyprium's asset transfer to Sentynl. And remember, FBIO owns around 73.9% of Cyprium's equity. This means FBIO gained a potentially very valuable asset with that PRV, because PRVs have historically been sold between $21.2 million and $350.0 million.

Of course, that's a big price range, but compared to FBIO's market cap of just $108.6 million, even a relatively cheap price tag for its PRV would be material for investors. And, also remember that from 2020 to 2024, the average PRV traded at around $100.0 million. So, if FBIO monetizes its PRV at a similar price tag, it could easily be a transformative upside catalyst for the stock.

Conclusion: Worth Speculating

Overall, FBIO's business model is unique in its sector. They're mostly a holding company of sorts in biotech, despite the fact that they also conduct in-house R&D. But more recently, in January 2026, Cyprium's asset transfer to Sentynl created a major upside catalyst for FBIO in the near term. After all, they can now theoretically find a buyer for their newly acquired PRV and quickly monetize that into a sizeable payday for investors. Of course, this is a speculative thesis for now because we don't have any concrete disclosures or timetables regarding when they'll sell their PRV. But, reasonably speaking, I would imagine selling their PRV is high on management's priorities now, as it could quickly unlock a lot of shareholder value. But there's always the risk that the PRV sale drags on and fails to materialize, and in the meantime, FBIO continues burning through its cash reserves. That's why you should only think of FBIO as a small speculative biotech play for the foreseeable future.

data.title: Wendy's: The Market Hates The Reset - I'm Buying It
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered The Wendy's Company (WEN), I highlighted their revitalization efforts amid significant macro pressure, with hundreds of underperforming store closures in an effort to revitalize their brand while focusing on quality, marketing, and their international expansion.

Wendy's latest report continued this turnaround in the same direction, which the market clearly did not like, as the stock is down more than 20% since publishing the previous article, but I believe this warrants an upgrade to Strong Buy now, as Wendy's turnaround potential and value are now even more compelling, with a very strong and sustainable dividend on top.

Internal Developments

WEN's Q4 report was better than the market expected, beating on both top- and bottom-line estimates, although they still reported significant drops in the US (10.5% systemwide sales decline) despite a 6.2% international systemwide sales increase - opening 59 new international restaurants - while macro pressure is expected to continue.

As for the free cash flow, we can see them delivering $205.4 million in 2025, a 13.6% decrease compared to $237.7 million in 2024, which is still solid for a ~$1.29 billion market cap at the moment of writing this, placing them at a very attractive P/FCF ratio of ~6.3.

As for their 2026 guidance, Wendy's expects relatively flat global systemwide sales growth, with an Adjusted EBITDA of $460 million to $480 million and a total CAPEX of $120 million to $130 million (vs. ~$140 million in 2025), with a free cash flow dropping to $190 million to $205 million.

Meanwhile, their Adjusted EPS is seen dropping to $0.56 to $0.60 in 2026, well below the market's expectations, which, combined with their downsizing plan to focus on value enhancements mentioned below, led to significant target price cuts and drops in their stock price.

This is part of a wider operational refresh plan called Project Fresh, which also includes between 5% and 6% US restaurant closures in H1 2026, redirecting capital towards AUV initiatives, better marketing, a new menu framework, and a focus on their value/quality differentiators while also continuing their international expansion.

They also plan to keep paying a dividend, reiterating their commitment to it and recently declaring a $0.14 quarterly dividend (in line with previous), sustaining a roughly 8.25% dividend yield, which currently costs about $106.36 million, which is very sustainable even if their FCF drops, leading to a ~53.85% payout ratio based on their 2026 guidance's midpoint.

To be fair, I'd rather see a new CEO (they currently have an interim one) cut the dividend and dedicate a majority of these funds to their "rebuilding year," potentially even building a quality logistics system, regaining consumer trust, and even buying back shares. I like the store closures as long as they focus on the below-optimal ones (which seems to be the case), and this could be a strong step towards a potential recovery, which the company's CFO and Interim CEO also mentioned during their Q4 earnings call:

Turning to system optimization, which is about having the right footprint in each market to improve franchisee economics and enhance the customer experience. By closing consistently underperforming restaurants, we are enabling our franchisee partners to increase focus on locations with the greatest potential for profitable growth. Since we announced this program in November, we have been working with our franchisees to evaluate restaurants on a store-by-store basis and make collaborative decisions to optimize performance across the U.S. system as One Wendy's.

Financially, based on WEN's latest report, we can see an overall normal position for the industry, with the current assets now covering their current liabilities once again, and even though the debt is high, it's spread very well over the next more than half a decade, with no significant maturity that can't be refinanced, which should also buy them a good amount of time to wait for better conditions before refinancing.

Macro Developments

Especially in this case, I believe it's important to reiterate that we're not talking (only) about company-specific issues, but rather trends affecting not only the fast-food industry, but virtually everything that relies on consumers.

This weak consumer environment is normal in a high-rate market, as the role of rate hikes is to slow down the economy by lowering discretionary budgets, and fast-food is one of the first things I'd consider when tightening the belt.

As we can see based on very recent data, US consumer confidence collapsed to the lowest level since 2014, while about 32% of Americans have reportedly traded down even the most basic necessities such as food, which likely has an even bigger impact on the QSR industry since it's one of the most discretionary branches of it, which is very much visible for Wendy's - alongside concerns surrounding their quality.

In WEN's defense, they are addressing this environment as best as they can, with both better value propositions (see the new value deals above) and a focus on closing underperforming locations to focus on improving their brand image.

However, this normal weakness has been accentuated by a variety of factors affecting the dollar and US treasuries, which can in turn impact Wendy's and its consumers, including tariffs, the weakening dollar, crisis worries, Japan's economic plans that can threaten the dollar and treasuries even more - just to name a few of the headwinds coming in recent weeks - all of which can lead to even more pressure for longer, despite recent economic data boosting the market's confidence.

Another point I'd like to address is the GLP-1 threat, with recent data highlighting that households where at least one person took the medicine saw a 5.5% decrease in grocery spending six months after it, with the biggest drops seen in the calorie-dense products such as sweets and snacks, with the report highlighting that "primary dietary change appears to be a reduction in high-calorie items."

Risks Reiterated

Although I don't see GLP-1 by itself as such a big threat given the affordability and demand (5.5% drop in grocery spending if 100% of households took it is not much, and it's impossible to see this kind of adoption), I also believe that this can be just one step towards broader fundamental changes, with potential effects MAHA initiatives, GLP-1, and overall more health-conscious consumer trends, potentially leading to a mix of lower volumes and higher costs, as WEN would have to adapt to potential long-term changes.

Meanwhile, WEN has to fight in a very competitive environment, with several big names and many other contenders competing for the same limited consumer budgets, while their ownership structure can also be an issue (which has been the case in the past), as mentioned in the previous analysis, while on the day after writing this, we saw Nelson Peltz disclosing in an SEC filing that he is considering what to do with his 16% stake in WEN.

Valuation

For WEN's DCF to Equity valuation, I'll start with their $205.4 million FCF in 2025 and assume a midpoint of $197.5 million in 2026 based on their guidance (note that I'm not accounting for the net borrowings since I want to highlight their true cash flow generating potential), followed by a CAGR of 7% this time to account for more hurdles but still solid potential to recover and expand internationally, especially if the consumer environment also improves (well below the market's EPS estimates), followed by a CAGR of 5% for the subsequent 5 years, a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a very high 15% discount rate to account for competitive pressure and broader macro risks, with 190.54 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $1.91 billion, for an intrinsic value of $10.03 per share, which is well above the current $6.75 at the moment of writing this despite the very high discount rate. Even if we assume significantly lower growth or even negative rates, we're still not far from the current level, which can at the very least suggest their value even as a cigar butt type of investment, while more optimistic scenarios can easily get us to the levels from a couple of years ago.

Conclusion

I'm upgrading WEN to a Strong Buy now, with the stock trading at a deep discount while the company continues to produce very strong cash flows and can even multiply if they deliver a recovery.

Again, there are risks to keep in mind, and this turnaround can obviously fail, but even as a cigar butt investment, there is still plenty of value here. I placed an order to open a position to take advantage of it and I'm looking forward to even better prices while the company works on its turnaround and the environment advances in a better direction.

data.title: NVDL: Trading On Share Price Volatility Going Into The Next Bull Wave
data.description:
GraniteShares 2x Long NVDA Daily ETF (NVDL) is a leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide 2x the daily performance of Nvidia (NVDA) shares. With Nvidia reporting Q4’26 earnings on February 25, 2026, I believe there may be opportunity to earn into the close of the fiscal year by strategically utilizing NVDL. Given the substantial risks involved in trading leveraged strategies, NVDL should only be used by experienced traders and for daily exposure; holding leveraged ETFs for longer than a single trading day may expose investors to compounded risk, NAV erosion, and unsalvageable losses if a trader were to go south. As a disclosure, I recently reiterated my Strong Buy rating for NVDA shares.

You can review my Q4’26 earnings preview for Nvidia here:

“Nvidia: Closing Out FY26 With A Robust Growth Opportunity (Earnings Preview)”

Setting the Stage

NVDA shares have been relatively range-bound since August 2025; I believe price action has largely been tied to uncertainty as to whether an AI bubble will burst and if Nvidia will maintain its leadership as the go-to AI accelerator stack.

I believe the AI bubble is underpinned by a number of different factors, including:

  1. High capital investment outlay for data center builds.
  2. Accelerated depreciation & amortization expense.
  3. Uncertainty as to whether enterprise AI will be adopted.
  4. Profitability, or lack thereof, by the AI developers.
  5. Novelty of AI.
  6. Circular investment cycle between the cloud services providers, semiconductor developers, and AI developers.

Starting at the top of the list, the first two factors are set to benefit Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) over time given the continued investment cycle in building out compute capacity tied with the replacement of older GPUs & CPUs. I believe this can lead to compounded growth in which the serviceable market is expanding and will inevitably need to be replaced. This means that Nvidia’s growth doesn’t necessarily end when the final data center is constructed, as older server racks will eventually be replaced with newer, faster, and more energy-efficient infrastructure.

The 3rd factor on the list, enterprise adoption, has been a slow-burn growth factor. I believe the market overestimated the rate of AI development to adoption, particularly given that agentic AI is designed to augment or replace human capital. With the majority of the enterprise market in the pilot phase for AI adoption, I believe there will inevitably be a ramp-up, just not as quickly as the market may be anticipating. I believe this coincides with Apple’s (AAPL) slow approach to adopting AI across its mobile devices, in which enterprises cannot risk AI applications disrupting operations if they do not function as intended. For a large enterprise, it can take multiple years to roll out an ERP system, and these systems aren’t necessarily a novel platform by comparison. I believe what’s typically overlooked is the complexity of internal processes that may result in slower agentic AI adoption, which I expect to reflect the timeframe for an ERP rollout.

The final point is the profitability of AI developers. The two major independent model developers, OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO), remain in the pre-profitability stage as they continue to develop more advanced model capabilities while scaling their user bases. Accordingly, Anthropic is expected to break even by 2028, while OpenAI’s path to profitability is anticipated to occur in the 2030 timeframe. I don’t believe this is necessarily a roadblock, given that these companies are positioned for high-growth/high-adoption rates rather than targeting profitability. One must bear in mind that AI is a capital-intensive business that requires a significant amount of foundational spending before realizing revenue, similar to the oil & gas shale boom in the 2010s; oil producers were capital-intensive with steep decline curves and didn’t necessarily focus on cash flow until much later in the growth cycle. I believe the AI development market is reflective of the US oil fracking market, where a substantial amount of capital is required to ramp up production before realizing a net profit.

All this relates to Nvidia; each of these factors is supportive of Nvidia’s growth trajectory, as the common theme is that more GPU & CPU capacity will be needed for continued AI development and deployment.

One theme that may be more specific to Nvidia is whether the semiconductor developer will maintain its leadership as more custom silicon designs hit the market. For example, Alphabet (GOOG) and Amazon (AMZN) each have multi-billion-dollar agreements with Anthropic for custom silicon chips and data center capacity. Though I don’t believe these deals will necessarily displace Nvidia, I can see the concerns they may raise for investors looking for a reason to turn bearish on Nvidia.

My last point on the case for Nvidia is the increasing capital outlay for compute capacity amongst the hyperscalers. I’m forecasting a 76% year-over-year growth rate for capital deployed during 2026, summing to $666 billion in spend, which is expected to largely be tied to CPUs & GPUs. I believe this should be taken as a positive indicator for the future of Nvidia’s growth trajectory given the expansive investment outlays for compute capacity. Given that GPUs have an accelerated depreciation cycle, I’m also expecting a certain degree of GPU replacement to occur going forward.

Strategically, investors may trade NVDL through the market uncertainty, particularly given NVDA shares’ range-bound trading pattern. Given the market’s directional uncertainty, traders can position through the volatility cycles, seeking price squeezes as investor sentiment turns from negative to positive. Given my positive outlook for NVDA shares, I believe NVDL can be an appealing trading strategy, as the underlying shares may present upward momentum as more clarity is brought to the market in the upcoming Q4’26 earnings release.

About GraniteShares 2x Long NVDA Daily ETF

NVDL was launched by GraniteShares on December 13, 2022 on the NASDAQ Exchange. NVDL has a net expense ratio of 105 bps and is aligned with peer leveraged strategies.

NVDL exhibits substantial liquidity with $3.8b in net assets and an average of $932mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, making it the most liquid leveraged NVDA ETF on the market.

Risks Related to NVDL

NVDL employs swap derivatives in order to gain 200% exposure to NVDA shares, creating certain intricacies that investors must consider when trading this ETF. For example, if NVDA shares appreciate by 5% in a single day, NVDL will appreciate by 10%; the same is true to the downside, in which if NVDA shares decline by -5% in a single trading day, NVDL shares will decline by -10%. Swaps are derivative contracts that provide exposure to the performance of the underlying asset in exchange for a fixed rate. Swaps are settled on a daily basis, with performance reset on the following trading day. This reset feature is reflected in the ETF, which may result in a compounding effect when held for longer than a single trading day. The compounding effect can significantly increase risk exposure, particularly to the downside if NVDA shares were to decline over consecutive trading days. Leveraged strategies also experience a certain degree of NAV erosion as a result of the daily rebalance that may most prominently impact long-term investors, making these strategies most appropriate for daily exposure.

For example, if the underlying index were to appreciate by 2% in a single day, a 2x strategy would appreciate by 4%, meaning that an investment of $100 would increase to $104 the same trading day. If the following day results in a -2% decline in the underlying index, or a -4% decline in the leveraged strategy, the investment will decline to $99.84 the same trading day.

Traders may experience higher short-term capital gains taxes on top of exchange fees and the expense ratio, potentially impacting the total returns to the trader.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Final Thoughts

NVDL can be an appealing trading strategy for those seeking to benefit from the positive outlook for NVDA shares while incrementally trading on short-term volatility. Given the outlook for NVDA, I am recommending NVDL with a Buy rating for enhancing daily exposure. Investors could potentially utilize NVDL for longer holding periods, though they should consider the involved risks prior to considering this option.

data.title: American Express Outlook Remains Positive Despite Recent Volatility
data.description:
American Express (AXP) still looks like a pretty solid investment to me and the past few quarters have only reinforced that view. Since my last Buy call, the stock's basically gone nowhere. It did climb to a new high around $387, but then gave those gains back after President Donald Trump floated the idea of a one-year cap on credit card interest rates at 10% and the Credit Card Competition Act. Even so, the stock is still up roughly 10% over the past year overall. I did mention before that the price was a bit steep, but the company's results and higher earnings show that paying up made sense here.

Premium Customers Continue to Drive Growth

A big reason for this is the kind of customers Amex has. The card base is mostly affluent and pretty steady, over 151 million cards now and a lot of the new accounts are for their premium cards. Amex has been putting money into making its cards feel more high-end and it's working. Card fee income has grown 17% a year since 2019. Just in Q4, card fee revenues were up 17% from the year before. Management expects card fees to keep growing in the mid- to high-teens as the new Platinum pricing settles in. They're really pushing Platinum and other premium cards. You can see it not just in the revenue numbers, but in how much more Platinum cardholders spend. According to management, Platinum accounts spend about ten times what a basic Blue Cash card does. This isn't just a vanity stat, it means higher average revenue per account and it costs less for every dollar spent.Operating leverage has improved as premium products scale, helping offset higher marketing and product investments.

Spending Growth Shows Strong Customer Engagement

The numbers from their network back this up. In Q4, total volume was up 9%. If you break it down, international card services went up 12% and U.S. consumer services were up 9%. Growth among younger customers remains strong, with Gen-Z spending growth particularly robust in reported segments. That's important because it shows Amex isn't just swapping out old customers for the same kind of people. They're actually bringing in younger, digital-first spenders. That should help keep card fees and network volumes growing and it answers the worry that Amex's customers are getting too old.

Credit Quality and Returns Remain Strong

Credit performance keeps holding up AXP's better-than-average returns. Net write-offs sit at 2.1%. Delinquency ratios are at 1.3%, which is lower than what most of the industry sees. That shows AXP is still picking customers who usually pay their bills and keeps hanging on to people with good credit. Return on equity came in at about 34%. The CET1 ratio sticks within management's 10-11% target range, so they've got room to keep buying back stock and bump the dividend 16% to $0.95 a share.

Earnings Growth and Guidance Support the Outlook

Management bumped up their medium-term revenue growth outlook to 9-10% for 2026. They're calling for EPS between $17.30 and $17.90. Consensus lands at $17.55, which falls right in line with the trend. For all of 2025, AXP pulled in $72.2 billion in net revenue (that's up 10% over last year). Net income moved up 7% to $10.8 billion. Adjusted EPS grew 15% to $15.38. Looking to 2027, consensus has EPS getting to $20.09 on $86 billion in revenue. Even if sales growth cools down, strong margins and stable credit risk should still bring steady double-digit profit growth.

Right now, the stock trades at about 20 times forward earnings. That's not cheap but that price makes sense: AXP keeps putting up returns on equity in the mid-30s and keeps beating on earnings. This price already assumes the company will keep growing at a double-digits pace. Still, the latest guidance and growth among younger customers and in international markets mean there's a chance results could beat what analysts expect, especially if younger and global users keep coming in at the rates we've seen. If management keeps hitting their marks, EPS might go above $20 and help both with buybacks and pushing the share price higher.

Risks Exist but the Growth Story Remains Intact

What really makes AXP stand out is that there aren't many true substitutes for what they do: their model mixes spending-based revenue, premium card fees and the ability to pull value from both their own products and partner cards. The engine for card fee growth still has a clear path as they roll out the new Platinum card. Management keeps pushing for better digital customer experiences too, including new AI projects and more international merchants. These could add extra revenue sooner than most people expect.

Regulators are pushing harder lately. Stuff like political talk around rate caps and the Credit Card Competition Act, those are getting louder and they could hit the business in a real way. Sudden drops in how confident people feel or what's happening with jobs could hurt, too. If rules come in that cut into card fees or squeeze interchange, it could really push down earnings power if the government moves fast. If revenue growth drops below 8%, credit losses drift up and fees start getting squeezed, Wall Street would likely have to cut its EPS forecasts to under $16. That could push the stock's high-end valuation down by 15-20%. For now though, credit quality actually looks better, December numbers show that net charge-offs and late payments are ticking down.

Investors are optimistic, but not getting carried away. The stock jumped after Q3's strong results, but didn't really move much when Q4 earnings missed (mostly because of higher spending and not as many new cards signed up). That shows investors are aware there's risk in running the business well, even if the company keeps beating the competition. If you're going to argue against being bullish, you'd need to see credit trends really get worse, people stop signing up for premium cards, or regulators move fast and actually pass tough new rules. Right now, none of that's happening. As long as management keeps doing what they're doing, risk and reward are still tilted in favor of owning the stock. The upside isn't just about the stock price going up, it's about Amex being able to keep growing earnings by over 10% and grabbing more revenue from both fees and the network. If revenue or earnings start missing, or if new rules show up and start to clearly drag down profits, then it's time to rethink. But for now, those problems aren't showing up.

I'm sticking with a Buy on American Express. They lead the premium card space, keep putting up strong numbers, the stock's not overpriced and there's a good chance for more upside. The company keeps finding ways to make more money from both old and new customers and they're controlling costs and keeping credit losses low. If Amex can't keep growing card fees, expand globally, or keep credit quality high, or if new regulations actually start hurting profits, I'll change my view. For now, though, the risk-reward setup still looks pretty favorable.
